model_narrative: We route a single commodity through a directed network from one source node to a set of demand nodes over a fixed set of existing arcs. For each arc we decide how much flow to send across it and whether to open the arc at all. Sending flow incurs a variable cost per unit carried, and opening an arc incurs a separate one-time fixed cost that is charged whenever the arc is used. The objective is to minimize the total cost, combining the variable flow cost over all arcs with the fixed opening cost of every arc that is opened.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the network nodes; one distinguished node acts as the single source that supplies the commodity and the others are demand nodes that consume it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the sub-arc label distinguishing parallel arcs between the same ordered pair of nodes; here there is a single label so at most one arc runs in each direction between any two nodes

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: the existing directed arcs over which flow may be routed, each identified by an origin node, a destination node, and a sub-arc label; this is a sparse three-dimensional set listing only the arcs that physically exist

params.name: vcost
params.doc: the variable cost charged per unit of flow sent over an arc, a single scalar that applies uniformly to every arc

params.name: fcost
params.doc: the one-time fixed cost incurred for opening an arc, charged in full whenever the arc carries any flow, regardless of how much

params.name: xupp
params.doc: the maximum amount of flow an arc may carry, equal to the total supply available at the source so that a single arc could in principle carry the entire commodity

params.name: demand
params.doc: the net demand of each node, defined as consumption minus production; it is positive at demand nodes, negative at the single source node where the magnitude equals the total supply, and zero at pure transshipment nodes

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount of flow routed over each arc, a continuous nonnegative quantity

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the open/closed decision for each arc, equal to 1 when the arc is opened and incurs its fixed cost and 0 when it stays closed

constraints.name: x[m,
constraints.intent: At every node the flow must balance so that the total amount arriving minus the total amount leaving equals that node's net demand. For each node, add up the flow on all arcs coming into it, subtract the flow on all arcs going out of it, and require the result to match the net demand of that node, which draws flow toward the demand nodes and out of the source.

constraints.name: x[m,
constraints.intent: No flow may travel over an arc unless that arc has been opened, and even an opened arc cannot carry more than its capacity. For each arc, the flow on it must stay at or below its capacity when the arc is open, and must be forced to zero whenever the arc is closed.

query: Both n2 and n4 sit right next to the depot, yet the plan feeds at least one of them the roundabout way instead of a straight shot from n1. Shouldn't the depot just commit to opening direct lines to both of those nearby points rather than relaying their orders through the tree? What's the model seeing that makes those two direct lanes not worth lighting up together?

query: There's a proposal on the table to standardize the equipment so that no single link in the network ever carries more than three units of flow in a cycle. If we hold every arc's throughput to that uniform ceiling, what does the optimizer return for total build-and-haul cost?

query: The whole delivery network hangs off just a couple of lines leaving the depot, which feels fragile if one of them goes down. Wouldn't it be safer to make the depot fan its outbound shipments across at least three separate lines instead of leaning on two? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is making by keeping the depot's footprint that narrow?
expected_pyomo: model.spread_outbound = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y['n1', n, 'a1'] for n in model.n if ('n1', n, 'a1') in model.arc) >= 3)

query: Operations is pitching a more redundant routing plan that deliberately keeps more product moving across the network instead of running everything along the tightest possible paths. Say we required the total flow summed over all arcs to come to at least 70 units. What does the optimizer come back with for total network cost under that floor?
expected_pyomo: model.total_flow_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[m, n, sa] for (m, n, sa) in model.arc) >= 70)

query: We're looking at a permit regime that throttles every individual line leaving the depot to the same ceiling — no single corridor out of n1 may carry more than five units a cycle, so any heavier load has to be split across multiple outbound lanes. With that uniform per-lane limit on the depot's outbound arcs, what total cost does the optimizer return?

query: There's a proposal to give the n4 corridor a guaranteed share of the depot's dispatch so it doesn't get starved. The idea is that the direct line from n1 to n4 should always carry at least thirty percent of everything the depot ships out — measured against the total flow leaving n1 across all its outbound lines. Plug that share rule in and walk me through what it does to total network cost.
expected_pyomo: model.n4_min_share = Constraint(expr=model.x['n1', 'n4', 'a1'] >= 0.30 * sum(model.x['n1', n, 'a1'] for n in model.n if ('n1', n, 'a1') in model.arc))

query: Engineering is weighing a two-part service package for the nearby points: hold the direct n1-to-n4 lane down to no more than two units because of a weight restriction on that road, but at the same time guarantee that the depot ships at least four units total straight to n2 and n4 combined rather than relaying them. Apply both of those together and tell me what the optimizer returns for total cost.

query: We're weighing a single-feeder design where the depot commits to just one outbound line and everything fans out from there. If n1 is allowed to open at most one arc leaving it, what total cost does the model come back with?
expected_pyomo: model.single_feeder = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y['n1', n, 'a1'] for n in model.n if ('n1', n, 'a1') in model.arc) <= 1)

query: The nearby points n2, n3 and n4 all get fed the long way round instead of straight from the depot, which seems wasteful when they're right there. Shouldn't the depot be pushing a decent chunk of volume straight to those three on the direct lines — say at least seven units combined across the three direct lanes from n1? What's keeping the optimizer from serving them directly like that?

query: The plan lights up a lot of separate links to build out this tree, and every opened arc is a fixed charge we keep paying. Couldn't we run a leaner network and hold the total number of opened arcs across the whole network to no more than sixteen? What's the model getting out of keeping that many links live instead of consolidating?
expected_pyomo: model.arc_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[m, n, sa] for (m, n, sa) in model.arc) <= 16)

sets.doc: the sub-arc label distinguishing parallel arcs between the same ordered pair of nodes; here there is a single label so at most one arc runs in each direction between any two nodes

sets.name: arc
sets.doc: the existing directed arcs over which flow may be routed, each identified by an origin node, a destination node, and a sub-arc label; this is a sparse three-dimensional set listing only the arcs that physically exist

params.name: vcost
params.doc: the variable cost charged per unit of flow sent over an arc, a single scalar that applies uniformly to every arc

params.name: fcost
params.doc: the one-time fixed cost incurred for opening an arc, charged in full whenever the arc carries any flow, regardless of how much

params.name: xupp
params.doc: the maximum amount of flow an arc may carry, equal to the total supply available at the source so that a single arc could in principle carry the entire commodity

params.name: demand
params.doc: the net demand of each node, defined as consumption minus production; it is positive at demand nodes, negative at the single source node where the magnitude equals the total supply, and zero at pure transshipment nodes

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the amount of flow routed over each arc, a continuous nonnegative quantity

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the open/closed decision for each arc, equal to 1 when the arc is opened and incurs its fixed cost and 0 when it stays closed

constraints.name: x[m,
constraints.intent: At every node the flow must balance so that the total amount arriving minus the total amount leaving equals that node's net demand. For each node, add up the flow on all arcs coming into it, subtract the flow on all arcs going out of it, and require the result to match the net demand of that node, which draws flow toward the demand nodes and out of the source.

constraints.name: x[m,
constraints.intent: No flow may travel over an arc unless that arc has been opened, and even an opened arc cannot carry more than its capacity. For each arc, the flow on it must stay at or below its capacity when the arc is open, and must be forced to zero whenever the arc is closed.

query: The depot relays a lot of the demand nodes through intermediate hops instead of serving them itself, and that routing-through feels like it's hiding the real delivery cost. Wouldn't it be more honest to guarantee every demand point gets at least one unit shipped to it straight from the depot on its own direct lane? What's the tradeoff the optimizer is dodging by relaying them instead?
